one_line_takeaway: A small-scale empirical study showing that wrapping LLM agents in an external safety filter, an internal safety-reviewer agent, or per-step hierarchical safety consultation all substantially reduce unsafe outputs (best: filter and hierarchical), at the cost of latency and with residual jailbreak vulnerability.
key_finding: All three frameworks sharply improved safety over the unsafe baseline (baseline scores 1.31-1.87 for text). The LLM input-output filter and the hierarchical process performed best, refusing/handling nearly all prompts; the hierarchical framework gave the most comprehensive safe answers (often offering safe alternatives instead of flat refusal) and the LLM filter mostly just refused. The dedicated safety agent performed decently but had lapses, especially in Guns & Illegal Weapons, sometimes passing generalized-but-risky content. GPT-4o was the best overall backbone; on the unsafe baseline GPT-4o and GPT-3.5 actually produced MORE unsafe Guns & Violence responses than Llama3.1-8b/Gemma 2. The safety-agent image-generation variant refused all malicious image prompts (2.20 baseline -> 3.00). The hierarchical framework's cost is ~2-3 extra minutes of latency per answer plus higher compute.
core_contribution: The paper proposes, implements, and comparatively evaluates THREE safety architectures for LLM-powered AI agent systems: (1) an LLM-powered input-output filter, in which an external GPT-4o-based safety LLM sits as a 'shield' around the agent, screening every input before it reaches the agent and every output before it reaches the user, and blocking the entire prompt/output if unsafe; (2) a dedicated safety agent integrated INSIDE the multi-agent system as an internal reviewer that inspects the support agent's generated output and edits/redacts only the unsafe portions rather than blocking wholesale (also extended to a multimodal image-generation variant that vets prompts before a DALL-E image agent); and (3) a hierarchical delegation-based system in which a manager agent consults the safety agent between EVERY inter-agent delegation step (manager->research agent->support agent->manager->user), giving comprehensive intermediate-step oversight rather than only input/output filtering. The frameworks are built on a single reference 'customer support' agent system. Note: LlamaGuard, ToxicChat, Azure AI Content Safety, and the OpenAI Moderation API appear only as cited Related Work / category sources, NOT as the authors' own systems or evaluation datasets.
threat_model: Adversarial users attempting to elicit unsafe or harmful outputs/actions from a deployed LLM agent system via jailbreaking (hypothetical/role-play framing, keyword modification, cloaking a malicious request inside a larger benign prompt). Harm categories tested: Hate & Harassment, Guns & Illegal Weapons/Violence, Regulated/Controlled Substances, Suicide & Self-Harm, and Criminal Planning, plus unsafe image-generation requests. The paper situates agent-system risk between Anthropic's ASL-2 and ASL-3 levels. The attacker interacts with the agent at runtime; the defense goal is preventing harmful content/actions from being produced or returned, not detecting them offline.
limitations: (1) Very small, hand-built evaluation set of only 21 prompts, which the authors admit may not reflect real safety capability at scale. (2) Only a single agent-system type (a customer-support tool) was used. (3) Manual, coarse 0-3 safety scoring introduces subjectivity and no inter-rater reliability is reported. (4) Safety architectures were still circumventable in testing via fictional/'imagine' framing or by asserting importance/dependence, allowing weapon/harmful-substance instructions through. (5) The hierarchical framework is expensive and slow (2-3 min extra) and hardest to implement. (6) No existing agent-safety benchmark was used (none exists), limiting comparability; results are from the authors' own prompt set only. (7) No statistical significance testing given the tiny sample.

datasets_used: Authors' own curated set of 21 malicious text prompts across 5 categories (Hate & Harassment, Guns & Illegal Weapons/Violence, Regulated/Controlled Substances, Suicide & Self-Harm, Criminal Planning), released at github.com/ishaandomkundwar/Agent-Safety
A separate set of malicious image-generation prompts
(Cited only, NOT used for evaluation: ToxicChat, OpenAI Moderation Evaluation Dataset)
quantitative_results: Text safety score (0-3), No Safety baseline: GPT-4o 1.87, GPT-3.5-Turbo 1.31, Llama3.1-8b 1.69, Google Gemma 2 1.81
Text, LLM Filter: GPT-4o 2.94, GPT-3.5-Turbo 2.75, Llama3.1-8b 2.88, Google Gemma 2 2.94
Text, Safety Agent: GPT-4o 2.81, GPT-3.5-Turbo 2.63, Llama3.1-8b 2.69, Google Gemma 2 2.75
Text, Hierarchical Process: GPT-4o 3.0, GPT-3.5-Turbo 2.81, Llama3.1-8b 2.88, Google Gemma 2 2.94
Image Generation Attacks (GPT-4o + DALL-E): No Safety 2.20, Safety Agent 3.00 (LLM Filter and Hierarchical not applied to image task)
Hierarchical framework added ~2-3 minutes of extra latency per answer vs. the safety-agent framework
baselines_compared: 'No Safety' agent system (the unsafe baseline support agent, scores 1.31-1.87 text / 2.20 image)
The three proposed frameworks compared head-to-head (LLM Filter vs. Safety Agent vs. Hierarchical Process)
Cross-model comparison as backbone: GPT-4o, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Llama3.1-8b, Google Gemma 2 (and GPT-4o+DALL-E for images)
